article_title: SpaceX Scrubs First Starship V3 Launch After Hydraulic Failure, Sets May 22 Window for Flight 12
summary: Article approved with corrections: One minor factual error in the Fram2 crew count description (article says 3 crew members; correct total is 4 including Wang).

findings.message: Fram2 crew count misstated as three; source indicates four total
findings.details: The article states Chun Wang commanded 'a three and a half day polar orbit flight with three crew members,' but Universe Today (source-6) reports the Fram2 mission sent 'him and three other crew members' into polar orbit — meaning the total crew was four, not three.

content_preview.title: SpaceX Scrubs First Starship V3 Launch After Hydraulic Failure, Sets May 22 Window for Flight 12
content_preview.summary: A hydraulic pin failure on the launch tower arm forced SpaceX to scrub the first Starship V3 test flight at T-40 seconds, with a second attempt targeting 6:30 p.m. EDT on May 22.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

SpaceX called off its first attempt to launch the Starship Version 3 megarocket on Thursday evening after a mechanical failure on the launch tower prevented the countdown from completing. The scrub, which occurred at T-40 seconds during what would have been Starship's twelfth integrated test flight, was attributed by Chief Executive Elon Musk to a single faulty component. "The hydraulic pin holding the tower arm in place did not retract," Musk wrote on X, as reported by [Spaceflight...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured and thorough news article covering the Starship V3 scrub, vehicle upgrades, mission objectives, IPO stakes, and newly announced Moon/Mars missions. Word count (1089) is appropriate for the News category. Clear section organization.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 7 sources verified by reading local snapshots. source-0 (Spaceflight Now): confirmed scrub at T-40s, Gulf of Mexico booster splashdown, hydraulic pin quote from Musk, payload details, and $15B investment figure. source-1 (Space.com live updates): confirmed 6:30 p.m. EDT May 22 window, 90-minute window duration, Dan Huot quote, 7-month gap since last flight. source-2 (TechCrunch): confirmed $11B Starlink revenue, IPO filing context, hydraulic pin scrub; note TechCrunch incorrectly says 'Atlantic Ocean' for booster splashdown — article correctly cites Gulf per Spaceflight Now. source-3 (SpaceNews): confirmed V3 upgrades (hot staging ring, 3 grid fins 50% larger, fuel transfer line), Mars flyby announcement, 2-year mission duration, Wang quote. source-4 (SpacePolicyOnline): confirmed circumlunar mission quote from SpaceX website, Tito background, scrub details, Wang and Bouvet Island. source-5 (Space.com Starship V3 explainer): confirmed 408ft height, 124m, 33 engines, clean-sheet redesign quote, Artemis 4 late 2028, Musk production pipeline quote, fuel transfer tube Falcon 9 comparison. source-6 (Universe Today): confirmed Wang background (F2Pool, Stakefish, Malta citizenship, born 1982), circumlunar within 200km, 4-crew Fram2 mission detail.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: One minor inaccuracy found: the article describes the Fram2 mission as having 'three crew members' when Universe Today (source-6) clearly states Wang 'sent him and three other crew members into polar orbit' — total crew was four. All other claims verified against cited sources. The booster splashdown location (Gulf of Mexico) is correct per Spaceflight Now and Space.com; TechCrunch's 'Atlantic Ocean' reference in source-2 is an error in TechCrunch, not in the article.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and factual throughout. No editorializing or loaded language. Properly attributes quotes and claims to specific sources.
editor_notes.originality: Fresh coverage of a breaking news event (May 21-22 scrub). Not a duplicate of any existing Machine Herald article.
concerns: The article states Fram2 was 'a three and a half day polar orbit flight with three crew members' but the mission had four crew members total (Wang plus three others, per Universe Today source-6). This is a recoverable factual error in a subordinate clause.
recommendations: Future submissions should double-check crew counts for commercial spaceflight missions, which are well-documented.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong, well-sourced news article with one minor recoverable factual error in the Fram2 crew count. The headline, summary, and lead are all accurate and well-supported. APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS with a corrections record documenting the crew count error.
